retrieved_docs.title: T-Cell Lymphoma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Retinoids help through their antiproliferative and antiapoptotic properties. Commonly used topical regimens are bexarotene (FDA-approved) and tazarotene. Severe hyperlipidemia and central hypothyroidism are side effects of bexarotene. Systemic retinoids include acitretin, isotretinoin, and bexarotene. Topical chemotherapy commonly used are mechlorethamine (nitrogen mustard), an alkylating agent, and carmustine. Systemic chemotherapy commonly used is methotrexate, pralatrexate, chlorambucil, gemcitabine, pegylated doxorubicin, and antimetabolite like fludarabine phosphate, 2-deoxycoformycin. PUVA inhibits DNA and RNA synthesis, but it will affect both normal and neoplastic cells; thus, the side effect profile is secondary malignancies of the skin. Histone deacetylase inhibitors (HDACi) are the newer group of therapeutic options available for the treatment of CTCL, which have both transcription-dependent and independent mechanisms of action for the expression of tumor suppressor genes. Vorinostat and romidepsin are FDA-approved, with an overall response rate of almost 50% with a tolerable side effect profile, the prominent one being cardiac arrhythmias from QTc prolongation. Targeted therapies include imiquimod (Toll-like receptor agonist), denileukin diftitox (recombinant fusion protein), alemtuzumab (a monoclonal antibody against CD52), and everolimus ( mTOR pathway target). Agents that are still undertrial are proteasome inhibitors like bortezomib. Allogenic stem cell transplant is used in advanced stages, usually appropriate for young patients with relapsing disease. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia -- Treatment / Management -- Bruton tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors
retrieved_docs.content: A combination of venetoclax plus obinutuzumab is particularly used in patients in whom ibrutinib is contraindicated, such as patients with multiple comorbidities (example, history of severe bleeding, hepatic impairment, or atrial fibrillation) or if on any anticoagulation medications.  Studies have shown that the combination of venetoclax plus obinutuzumab demonstrates higher PFS and OS when compared with chlorambucil plus obinutuzumab. [63] [64]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia With Variant Genetics -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: Research is still ongoing concerning optimal treatment selection and sequencing of various options in the course of treatment. For example, one recent study evaluated patients in the early stage of chronic lymphocytic leukemia who were not in an active disease state. [73] The study focused on chemotherapy compared to no intervention but rather an observation. When used in this population, ibrutinib leads to a delayed onset of progression to the active stage of the disease. It also leads to a decreased necessity for patients requiring more targeted therapies in the future for CLL.

retrieved_docs.title: Lymphoma -- Treatment / Management -- Diffuse Large B- Cell Lymphoma
retrieved_docs.content: Clinical trials are currently underway to assess the efficacy and safety of adding immunomodulatory agents (lenalidomide) or Bruton’s tyrosine kinase inhibitors (ibrutinib) to R-CHOP in previously untreated DLBCL. [22] [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Plasmablastic Lymphoma -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: Several ongoing trials are pertinent to PBL. These are listed below, along with the phase and current status. Belantamab mafodotin in plasmablastic lymphoma and ALK+ large B-cell lymphoma (NCT04676360, phase 2, recruiting) Pomalidomide and dose-adjusted EPOCH with and without rituximab for HIV-associated lymphomas (NCT05389423, phase 1, recruiting) A study of daratumumab and dose-adjusted EPOCH in plasmablastic lymphoma (NCT04139304, phase 1, recruiting)

retrieved_docs.title: Relapsed and Refractory Follicular Lymphoma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: After 2 prior systemic therapies, the phosphoinositide 3'-kinase (PI3K) inhibitor copanlisib is approved by the FDA for use in relapsed or refractory follicular lymphoma. CHRONOS-3 trial randomly assigned 458 patients with relapsed clinically indolent non-Hodgkin lymphoma (275 with follicular lymphoma) in a 2:1 ratio to receive copanlisib plus rituximab versus placebo plus rituximab. [40] After a median follow-up of 19 months, copanlisib plus rituximab improved PFS (median 22 versus 14 months, HR 0.52, 95% CI 0.39-0.69). The addition of copanlisib increased serious treatment-emergent adverse events (47 versus 18 percent) with higher rates of grade 3/4 hyperglycemia and hypertension. High-dose chemotherapy with autologous HSCT, CAR-T, or other experimental treatments is recommended in patients with multiple relapses.

retrieved_docs.title: Mantle Cell Lymphoma -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Complications of the MCL due to the disease process itself based on the site of MCL involvement include splenic rupture due to splenomegaly, GI bleeding or obstruction or perforation due to MCL in the gastrointestinal tract, and tumor lysis in the blastoid variant of MCL are all the considerations. [34] Due to chemotherapy and stem cell transplants, complications of life-threatening infections, neutropenic sepsis, cytopenias needing transfusions, nausea, vomiting, and hair loss are possible. With targeted drug approvals in MCL with agents like ibrutinib, aBTK inhibitor, hypertension, arrhythmias, diarrhea, skin rashes, and bleeding risk are reported. These side effects are felt to be more related to off-target actions on EGFR, ITK, and PI3K. Second-generation BTK inhibitors like acalabrutinib and zanubrutinib are more specific to BTK and have fewer off-target side effects in terms of hypertension, arrhythmias, or rashes. [14] BCL-2 inhibitor venetoclax is now approved for MCL venetoclax drug tumor lysis syndrome, while lenalidomide edema, cytopenias, risk of blood clots, and diarrhea are expected complications and or side effects to be aware of. Brexucabtagene (CAR-T cell therapy ) is associated with encephalopathy, cytopenias, and pyrexia from cytokine release syndrome, which must be addressed during the treatments.

retrieved_docs.title: Mantle Cell Lymphoma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Ibrutinib, acalabrutinib, zanubrutinib, venetoclax +/- rituximab, bortezomib +/- rituximab, lenalidomide plus rituximab, ibrutinib and lenalidomide + rituximab and chimeric antigen receptor T-cells (CAR-T) received FDA approval and several other agents including BiTE antibodies, antibody-drug conjugates and are under clinical trials. BCR- B cell receptor, BTK - Bruton's tyrosine kinase

retrieved_docs.title: T-Cell Prolymphocytic Leukemia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: As stated before, eligible patients should be screened for entry into clinical trials of newer agents when possible. Recent clinical trials have shown some potential for a BCL-2 (B-cell Lymphoma 2) inhibitor, Venetoclax, in T-PLL. [14] BCL-2 is an anti-apoptotic gene that conveys chemoresistance. Its overexpression prolongs cancer cell survival. The addition of Ruxolitinib is thought to potentiate Venetoclax's action. It is a JAK1 and JAK2 inhibitor - blocking the ATP sites of these kinases. Additional studies should be forthcoming.

retrieved_docs.title: Adult T-Cell Leukemia -- Treatment / Management -- 3. Alemtuzumab
retrieved_docs.content: Alemtuzumab is a humanized monoclonal antibody against CD52 that has demonstrated clinical activity against chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL), cutaneous T-cell lymphoma (CTCL), and peripheral T-cell lymphoma (PTCL).  ATL cells express CD52 more frequently than other peripheral T cell lymphoma. Alemtuzumab, in combination with cyclophosphamide/doxorubicin/vincristine/prednisone (CHOP) regimen, was evaluated in 24 patients with peripheral T-cell lymphoma (PTCL) between 2003 and 2005. A total of 17 patients (71%) achieved complete remission, and the overall median duration of response was 11 months. [42] However, survival rates were unsatisfactory, and the risk of CMV infection limited its effectiveness. In a phase II study of 29 patients receiving single-agent alemtuzumab, the overall objective response rate was 52%. However, the median duration of response was only 14.5 months among the 15 responders, and the median OS was reported at 5.9 months. [43]

retrieved_docs.title: Gastric Lymphoma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Progressive gastric MALT (non-DLBCL) lymphoma after first-line rituximab-based chemotherapy can be treated with various salvage regimens with either rituximab + lenalidomide or oral BTK or PI3K-delta inhibitors. Clinical trial enrollment should always be considered in the relapsed or refractory setting. [33] Management of salvage therapy and clinical trials should always be guided by experienced hematologists at high-volume centers.

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia -- Treatment / Management -- B. Patients with Active Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Bruton tyrosine kinase inhibitors (ibrutinib, acalabrutinib) BCL-2 inhibitor (venetoclax) Purine analogs (fludarabine, pentostatin) Alkylating agents (cyclophosphamide, chlorambucil, bendamustine) Monoclonal antibodies (rituximab, ofatumumab, obinutuzumab) PI3K inhibitor (idelalisib) Choice of Therapy: The preferred initial treatment choice is based upon genetic risk stratification of the tumor along with patient fitness assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia -- Treatment / Management -- D. Relapsed/Refractory CLL
retrieved_docs.content: Ibrutinib is the first-line therapy to improve progression-free survival and response to treatment in relapsed/refractory CLL.  Other drugs of choice for relapsed CLL include idelalisib (a phosphoinositide 3'-kinase [PI3K] delta inhibitor), Alemtuzumab (a monoclonal antibody directed against CD52, a cellular marker found in CLL) and venetoclax (Bcl-2 inhibitor). Venetoclax is actively used in refractory CLL patients with 17p deletion. Venetoclax induces brisk apoptosis and occasionally induces tumor lysis syndrome. Anti-CD19 chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T-cell therapy has also been active in refractory cases. Anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody (rituximab, ofatumumab, obinutuzumab) may briefly alleviate symptoms in relapsed/refractory CLL patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Bortezomib -- Indications -- Non-FDA-Approved Uses
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment of antibody-mediated rejection in cardiac transplantation Relapsed or refractory cutaneous T-cell lymphomas Relapsed or refractory follicular lymphoma Relapsed or refractory peripheral T-cell lymphoma Systemic light-chain amyloidosis Relapsed or refractory Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1289
retrieved_docs.content: Ibrutinib is a targeted therapeutic agent that may be used to treat سرطـان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن (chronic lymphocytic leukemia), offering a pharmacologic option for managing this hematologic malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 214
retrieved_docs.content: The community describes blood cancer (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) as a group of leukemic disorders comprising lymphoid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي), which correspond to chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), respectively. Targeted therapy relies heavily on protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, imatinib mesylát, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—block the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase; the former four are used for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase inhibitors ibrutinib and zanubrutinib also belong to this class, with ibrutinib approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein kinase inhibitors include EGFR inhibitors canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, as well as other ATC‑listed agents cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin are employed in acute myeloid leukemia. Corticosteroids such as dexamethasone acetate (used for blood cancer and septic shock), dexamethasone phosphate, prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate demonstrate efficacy in hematologic malignancies. Chemotherapeutic agents irinotecan hydrochloride and pipobroman are also utilized. Contraindicated interventions include infection with human herpesvirus 1 and oncolytic viral therapy talimogene laherparepvec, which may exacerbate the disease. Collectively, these agents illustrate the role of kinase‑targeted and immunomodulatory drugs in the comprehensive management of leukemic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 70
retrieved_docs.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, include dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, comprise ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another protein kinase inhibitor subclass, consist of canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. By blocking EGFR tyrosine‑kinase signaling, these agents disrupt tumor cell proliferation and survival and can modulate immune responses, providing antineoplastic and immunomodulating effects. Collectively, targeted inhibition of BCR‑ABL, BTK, and EGFR exemplifies precision oncology, linking specific molecular targets to distinct hematologic and solid malignancies and guiding patient‑centered cancer therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 81
retrieved_docs.content: Blood cancer (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) encompasses leukemias, including lymphoid (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid (ابيضاض الدم النقوي) forms, with specific subtypes such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). Targeted kinase inhibition is a central therapeutic strategy. BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—are employed, the first three for lymphoid leukemia and the latter three for myeloid leukemia. BTK inhibitors, namely ibrutinib and zanubrutinib, belong to the same protein‑kinase inhibitor class, with ibrutinib indicated for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein‑kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity include EGFR inhibitors canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, as well as cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin are specifically used in acute myeloid leukemia. Conventional cytotoxic and corticosteroid agents such as cytarabine, dexamethasone acetate, dexamethasone phosphate, dexamethasonisonikotinát, irinotecan hydrochloride, pipobroman, and prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate also have activity against various blood cancers. Dexamethasone acetate additionally serves in the management of septic shock. Conversely, viral agents such as human herpesvirus‑1 and the oncolytic therapy talimogene laherparepvec are contraindicated in patients with blood cancer. These interrelated disease classifications, drug classes, and contraindications illustrate the complexity of therapeutic decision‑making in hematologic malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 48
retrieved_docs.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subgroup of protein kinase inhibitors classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, target the BCR‑ABL fusion protein. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are BCR‑ABL inhibitors indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are BCR‑ABL inhibitors used for myeloid leukemia. Bruton’s tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, include ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another subclass of protein kinase inhibitors, comprise canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, both belonging to the same antineoplastic and immunomodulating class. Cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin are members of the ATC subgroup “Other protein kinase inhibitors,” also classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, and gilteritinib and midostaurin have activity against acute myeloid leukemia. Collectively, these agents illustrate how precise molecular targeting of BCR‑ABL, BTK, EGFR and other kinases provides distinct therapeutic options across lymphoid, myeloid and chronic lymphocytic leukemias, underscoring the role of targeted kinase inhibition in modern oncology care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 75
retrieved_docs.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein‑kinase inhibitors classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, comprise dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي), whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي). Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein‑kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, include ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). The “Other protein kinase inhibitors in ATC” group, encompassing cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin, belongs to the same protein‑kinase‑inhibitor class and is employed to inhibit kinase signaling in cancer and to modulate immune responses. Gilteritinib and midostaurin are specifically recognized for the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). Collectively, these agents illustrate how distinct kinase‑targeted drugs are organized within broader therapeutic families and linked to the management of various hematologic malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1504
retrieved_docs.content: Dasatinib and imatinib are both tyrosine‑kinase inhibitor medications that may be used to treat ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي, a form of lymphoid leukemia. Both agents target abnormal signaling pathways in malignant lymphoid cells, offering therapeutic options for patients with this hematologic malignancy. Their use reflects the importance of targeted therapy in managing lymphoid leukemias and underscores the need for individualized treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 214
retrieved_docs.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors are classified as protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Within this class, ibrutinib and zanubrutinib are members; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors also belong to the broader category of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are specific EGFR inhibitors. Other protein kinase inhibitors listed in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification include cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin. Gilteritinib and midostaurin are indicated for the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), while cabozantinib is a member of this group with oncologic applications. Collectively, these agents illustrate how diverse protein kinase–targeting drugs—spanning BTK inhibitors, EGFR inhibitors, and other kinase inhibitors—are integrated into cancer therapy, each targeting specific malignancies and contributing to personalized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 765
retrieved_docs.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors are a class of targeted therapies that block the BTK enzyme, a key component of B‑cell receptor signaling. Ibrutinib and zanubrutinib are both members of this BTK inhibitor class, sharing the same mechanism of action. Ibrutinib may treat سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن (chronic lymphocytic leukemia) by inhibiting malignant B‑cell proliferation. Recognizing that both ibrutinib and zanubrutinib belong to the BTK inhibitor family informs clinicians when selecting appropriate targeted agents for patients with B‑cell malignancies such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1288
retrieved_docs.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors constitute a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors that possess antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Within this therapeutic class, zanubrutinib is a specific BTK inhibitor that exemplifies the targeted approach of these agents. By inhibiting BTK, these drugs are employed in the treatment of certain malignancies and immune‑mediated conditions, reflecting their role as both anticancer and immune‑modulating therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 83
retrieved_docs.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors constitute a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Within this subclass, ibrutinib and zanubrutinib are recognized members, and ibrutinib has therapeutic activity against chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Another grouping, designated as other protein kinase inhibitors in ATC, also belongs to the broader class of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin are members of this ATC group, and both gilteritinib and midostaurin are indicated for the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia. Together, these kinase‑targeting drugs illustrate the role of protein kinase inhibition in the management of hematologic malignancies such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia and acute myeloid leukemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 328
retrieved_docs.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors are a class of protein kinase inhibitors that also function as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Both ibrutinib and zanubrutinib are members of this BTK inhibitor class. Ibrutinib, as a BTK inhibitor, may be used in the treatment of chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 37
retrieved_docs.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein‑kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, include dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, imatinib mesylát, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib; they inhibit the BCR‑ABL tyrosine‑kinase pathway and are employed in hematologic malignancies, with dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib and imatinib mesylát indicated for lymphoid leukemia and ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib for myeloid leukemia. BTK inhibitors, another protein‑kinase inhibitor subgroup, comprise ibrutinib and zanubrutinib and target Bruton's tyrosine‑kinase; ibrutinib is approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia, highlighting BTK inhibition in lymphoid cancers. EGFR inhibitors such as canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab belong to the same protein‑kinase inhibitor class and exemplify kinase‑targeted therapy beyond leukemias. The ATC category “Other protein kinase inhibitors” also contains agents such as cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin have been identified as potential treatments for acute myeloid leukemia. Collectively, these protein‑kinase inhibitors—targeting BCR‑ABL, BTK, EGFR and other kinases—constitute a network of antineoplastic and immunomodulating drugs that underpin contemporary therapeutic strategies for lymphoid and myeloid leukemias and other cancers.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 483
retrieved_docs.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors constitute a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, and this class includes the drugs ibrutinib and zanubrutinib. Ibrutinib, as a member of the BTK inhibitor group, may be used to treat سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن (chronic lymphocytic leukemia). Similarly, Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors are also classified within the broader category of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents; this EGFR inhibitor group comprises agents such as canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. Together, BTK inhibitors and EGFR inhibitors illustrate how distinct targeted therapies share common mechanistic foundations as protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulatory effects, underscoring their relevance in the treatment of various cancers and immune‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 88
retrieved_docs.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors—including dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib—are classified as protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents; dasatinib, its anhydrous form and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are used for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors such as ibrutinib and zanubrutinib also belong to the protein kinase inhibitor, antineoplastic and immunomodulating group, with ibrutinib specifically indicated for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, exemplified by canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, are another subset of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Additional protein kinase inhibitors in this ATC subclass include cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin may be employed in the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia. Collectively, these kinase‑targeted therapies illustrate how distinct protein kinase inhibitor subclasses are organized within broader antineoplastic categories and matched to specific hematologic and lymphoid malignancies, supporting precise, patient‑centered treatment strategies.
